papers.title: Real-Time AI Service Economy: A Framework for Agentic Computing Across the Continuum
papers.innovation: Proposes a comprehensive framework for designing and operating real-time AI services across distributed computing environments (device-edge-cloud continuum), focusing on autonomous AI agents, multi-stage processing pipelines, resource competition, and policy-governed execution. This addresses critical architectural and operational challenges for AI deployment.
papers.benchmarks: Not explicitly mentioned in the abstract, but the framework directly addresses performance metrics like latency, resource utilization, and orchestration efficiency crucial for real-time distributed AI systems.
papers.use_case: Designing, deploying, and managing complex, latency-sensitive AI services and autonomous agents in distributed environments, such as smart cities, IoT applications, industrial automation, and edge computing infrastructures.

papers.title: The World Won't Stay Still: Programmable Evolution for Agent Benchmarks
papers.innovation: Challenges the static environment assumption in most existing benchmarks for LLM-powered agents. Introduces 'programmable evolution' for agent benchmarks, allowing for dynamic changes in environments, schemas, and toolsets to evaluate agent robustness and adaptability to real-world evolution.
papers.benchmarks: Proposes novel benchmarks featuring 'programmable evolution' to assess agent robustness to dynamic, changing environments, APIs, data schemas, and toolsets, moving beyond fixed evaluation scenarios.
papers.use_case: Developing, evaluating, and ensuring the long-term robustness and adaptability of LLM-powered agents in real-world, dynamic settings, where APIs change, data schemas evolve, and environments are non-static.

papers.title: Agentic LLM Planning via Step-Wise PDDL Simulation: An Empirical Characterisation
papers.innovation: Empirically characterizes the capability of Large Language Models (LLMs) to perform task planning for autonomous robotic systems. It leverages step-wise PDDL (Planning Domain Definition Language) simulation to assess LLMs as viable planners, bridging symbolic planning with modern LLM capabilities.
papers.benchmarks: Empirically characterizes LLM planning capabilities using step-wise PDDL simulation via PyPDDLEngine, providing a systematic comparison against classical symbolic planning methods for various planning tasks.
papers.use_case: Integrating LLMs into autonomous robotic systems for high-level task planning and action sequencing; developing agentic systems that can interpret goals and generate viable action plans in complex, goal-oriented environments.
